news_summary: The upcoming Artemis II mission will advance lunar exploration by conducting groundbreaking scientific research with human astronauts.
news_summary_fr: La prochaine mission Artemis II fera progresser l'exploration lunaire en menant des recherches scientifiques novatrices avec des astronautes humains.
news_summary_es: La próxima misión Artemis II impulsará la exploración lunar mediante la realización de investigaciones científicas pioneras con astronautas humanos.

articles.title: Artemis II mission is about to fly humans to the Moon — here’s the science they’ll do
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 31 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00964-4Set to lift off this week, the NASA flight will take astronauts around the Moon for the first time in more than 50 years.
articles.content:
The astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission will see more of the Moon’s far side by eye than any human has before.Credit: NASA/JPL

If all goes to plan, as soon as tomorrow, NASA will launch four people on a journey around the Moon. The mission, known as Artemis II, would be the first time humans have left Earth’s protective environment and travelled into deep space since the US Apollo programme, which ended more than half a century ago. And it could carry its astronauts farther from Earth than any humans have ever travelled.

Artemis II is one in a series of missions that ultimately aim to build humanity’s first permanent base on the Moon. This mission is supposed to test the rocket, crew capsule and other space-flight hardware that NASA wants to use to land humans on the lunar surface in the coming years. During their nearly ten-day journey to the Moon and back, astronauts plan to run experiments that will set the stage for future explorers.

“What we’re trying to do is not pick up where Apollo left off, but to use our decades of experience and knowledge and planning to do this sustainable presence on the Moon — and then to do science alongside of that,” says Barbara Cohen, a planetary scientist at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland.

The four Artemis II astronauts did a dress rehearsal in December for what will happen on launch day. Clockwise from front left are pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Jeremy Hansen, mission specialist Christina Koch and commander Reid Wiseman.Credit: Gregg Newton/AFP via Getty

Some of the key experiments that will be conducted during the Artemis II mission will explore how deep-space travel affects human health. Other research will rely on the astronauts’ ability to see geological features on parts of the Moon that have never been viewed by human eyes.

Cohen says the science findings from Artemis II will be fundamentally different from discoveries made previously by robots exploring the Solar System. “The amazing part of having crews is they have brains and eyes, and the capacity for thought and reaction,” so that they can “take the path of knowledge that is best for science”, she says.

The four astronauts who will fly aboard Artemis II have been through extensive science training, including field trips to sites in Canada and Iceland that are similar to the Moon’s otherworldly surface. One of the crew, Christina Koch, built space-science instruments before becoming an astronaut and has worked as a scientific field engineer in Antarctica and Greenland. Another, Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen, has a master’s degree in physics and worked in the underwater Aquarius laboratory off the coast of Florida.

“We’re excited about what the astronauts find interesting and what pulls their focus,” says Nicola Fox, head of NASA’s science mission directorate in Washington DC. “That is an incredible opportunity.”

Cells in space

One big focus for Artemis II will be to study how deep space affects human health. Studies of astronauts on short-duration space flights or in low Earth orbit on space stations have shown that space travel can raise the risk of cancer and induce vision problems, among other issues. Even the private space mission Inspiration4, which launched in 2021 and carried a crew led by Jared Isaacman — who is now the head of NASA — discovered health changes in the short, three-day spaceflight. The crew’s telomeres1, which are the protective ends of chromosomes, changed lengths.

But the Artemis II crew will be the first humans to expose their bodies to the deep-space radiation outside of Earth’s protective magnetic field since the final Apollo mission ended in 1972. Radiation sensors placed throughout their capsule’s cabin will measure exposure during the flight. And the astronauts will give saliva and blood samples before and after the mission, which researchers will check for alterations to the immune and other bodily systems.

But perhaps the most cutting-edge human-health study that will be conducted during Artemis II is an ‘organ on a chip’ experiment. For this, researchers asked each of the astronauts to donate platelets from blood before the space flight. From those donations, scientists extracted, isolated and froze immature bone marrow cells, which naturally circulate in people’s bloodstreams. Just before launch, the researchers plan to thaw and place the cells onto two chips, about the size of a USB drive, for each astronaut. One chip will fly aboard Artemis II, and the other will remain on Earth for the mission’s duration.

Researchers will place cells from each Artemis II astronaut onto an ‘organ on a chip’ to test their reaction to deep-space radiation during the flight.Credit: Emulate/NASA

Once the flight is over, researchers will compare both chips for each crew member to see if the cells that flew in space experienced more DNA damage, changes to telomere length or other signs of alteration owing to space flight2. That information can then be linked back to the particular astronaut and their health. “It’s the first time this has been done, and it’s all being done outside of low Earth orbit,” says biomedical scientist David Chou, the principal investigator for the organ-on-a-chip experiment at biotechnology company Emulate in Boston, Massachusetts.

If successful, such chips could help to protect astronauts in the future: NASA could simply fly chips containing cells from a potential astronaut into deep space to understand what might happen to them if they took such a journey.

A new view
articles.reasoning: The article describes the upcoming Artemis II mission, the first human deep-space mission around the Moon in over 50 years, which aims to test critical spaceflight technology and conduct pioneering scientific experiments on human health and lunar geology. This mission represents a significant step toward sustainable human presence on the Moon and advances scientific understanding with broad societal and exploratory benefits.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Artemis II mission prepares to send astronauts around the Moon for new science
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is about to launch Artemis II, a mission that will carry four astronauts on a trip around the Moon. This is the first time humans will travel beyond Earth’s protective environment into deep space since the Apollo missions ended over 50 years ago.

**What happened** – Artemis II is set to lift off soon with four astronauts onboard: Victor Glover, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch, and Reid Wiseman. The mission will last about ten days, testing new spaceflight technology like the rocket and crew capsule. During the trip, astronauts will perform experiments on how deep space affects human health and study parts of the Moon’s far side that no human has seen before.

**Impact** – This mission is important because it prepares the way for future Moon landings and building a permanent base there. Unlike robotic missions, having humans onboard means experiments can adapt quickly based on what astronauts observe. One key study will test how space radiation affects human cells using special “organ on a chip” technology. This could help protect astronauts on longer missions in the future.

**What’s next step** – After Artemis II, NASA plans to send astronauts to land on the Moon’s surface using the tested technology. The data from this mission’s health studies will guide how future crews stay safe during longer space trips. The mission also helps scientists learn more about the Moon’s geology, which will support building a lasting human presence.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II will send astronauts around the Moon to test new space technology and study how deep space affects humans, paving the way for future Moon exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA s’apprête à envoyer des astronautes autour de la Lune pour de nouvelles recherches
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA s’apprête à lancer Artemis II, une mission qui emmènera quatre astronautes faire un voyage autour de la Lune. C’est la première fois depuis plus de 50 ans que des humains quitteront l’environnement protecteur de la Terre pour aller dans l’espace lointain, depuis les missions Apollo.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Artemis II doit décoller bientôt avec à son bord Victor Glover, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch et Reid Wiseman. La mission durera environ dix jours et testera de nouvelles technologies spatiales comme la fusée et le module d’équipage. Pendant le voyage, les astronautes réaliseront des expériences sur l’impact de l’espace profond sur la santé humaine et étudieront des zones de la face cachée de la Lune jamais vues par des humains.

**Impact** – Cette mission est importante car elle prépare les futures missions d’atterrissage sur la Lune et la construction d’une base permanente. Contrairement aux robots, les humains peuvent adapter les expériences en fonction de leurs observations. Une étude clé testera les effets des radiations spatiales sur des cellules humaines grâce à une technologie appelée « organe sur puce ». Cela pourrait aider à protéger les astronautes sur de plus longs voyages.

**Prochaine étape** – Après Artemis II, la NASA prévoit d’envoyer des astronautes poser le pied sur la surface lunaire avec la technologie testée. Les données sur la santé permettront de mieux protéger les futures équipes lors de longs séjours dans l’espace. La mission aide aussi à mieux comprendre la géologie de la Lune, ce qui est essentiel pour y vivre durablement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Artemis II enverra des astronautes autour de la Lune pour tester de nouvelles technologies et étudier les effets de l’espace profond sur les humains, ouvrant la voie à l’exploration lunaire future.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA se prepara para enviar astronautas alrededor de la Luna para nueva ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está a punto de lanzar Artemis II, una misión que llevará a cuatro astronautas en un viaje alrededor de la Luna. Esta es la primera vez en más de 50 años que humanos viajarán fuera del entorno protector de la Tierra hacia el espacio profundo desde las misiones Apolo.

**Qué pasó** – Artemis II despegará pronto con los astronautas Victor Glover, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch y Reid Wiseman. La misión durará cerca de diez días y probará nueva tecnología espacial como el cohete y la cápsula de tripulación. Durante el viaje, los astronautas harán experimentos sobre cómo el espacio profundo afecta la salud humana y observarán partes de la cara oculta de la Luna que ningún humano ha visto.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es importante porque prepara el camino para futuras misiones de aterrizaje en la Luna y la construcción de una base permanente. A diferencia de los robots, los humanos pueden adaptar los experimentos según lo que vean. Un estudio clave probará cómo la radiación espacial afecta células humanas usando tecnología llamada «órgano en un chip». Esto podría ayudar a proteger a los astronautas en viajes más largos.

**Próximo paso** – Después de Artemis II, la NASA planea enviar astronautas a la superficie lunar usando la tecnología probada. Los datos sobre salud ayudarán a proteger a futuras tripulaciones en viajes largos. La misión también ayudará a aprender más sobre la geología lunar, apoyando una presencia humana duradera.

**Resumen en una frase** – Artemis II enviará astronautas alrededor de la Luna para probar nueva tecnología espacial y estudiar cómo el espacio profundo afecta a los humanos, preparando futuras exploraciones lunares.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a serene lunar landscape viewed from a spacecraft window, showing the far side of the Moon bathed in soft natural light, with four glowing, interconnected orbs symbolizing the Artemis II astronauts hovering gently above the surface; alongside, two delicate, translucent microchips float nearby, their intricate circuits glowing faintly to represent the organ-on-a-chip experiment studying human cells in deep space.
